text: This surgical technique video shows our robotic approach to a giant parasophageal hernia repair. The robotic platform offers the advantages of a binocular tridimensional visualization and wider range of motion and articulation in such small surgical fields. The procedure was performed by our Emory endosurgery team. We have performed more than fifty foregrip procedures robotically. We present a thirty-eight year old female with a history of delayed gastric emptying, heartburn, chronic cough, and frequent asthma exacerbations who failed medical management on PPI. A chest CT scan showed a seven centimeter parasophageal hernia.

This diagram represents our OR setup. We have tried different combinations, but this method has been the most reproducible and easiest for our OR team to learn. The patient is intubated in the regular position by the anesthesia staff. Once this is done, all tubing and monitoring devices are disconnected in order to turn the operating table a hundred and eighty degrees. These are then reconnected by both anesthesia and surgical teams and can be completed in about three minutes.

The patient's side cart is set for docking from above the patient's head. The surgeon is seated at the console, which is located at the right of the operating table. The first assistant stands at the patient left, guiding the robot arms into docking position. The surgeon is seated at the console, which is located at the right of the operating table. Vision cart and auxiliary monitors are placed at different angles facilitating the visualization to all the members of the team.

The surgeon uses the forearm da Vinci SI robot with three 8mm and two 12mm trocars. The patient is in a supine position with the table in reverse Trendelenburg and a slight right tilt. Arm number three serves as a liver retractor. The surgeon exposes the hiatus and reduces the hernia using a double fenestrated grasper in arm number two. The hernia sac is dissected with an advanced bipolar device, starting from the left side of the hiatus and separating it circumferentially from mediastinal and pleural attachments, taking care to avoid the pleural cavity.

The assistant retracts the stomach down and laterally while the surgeon divides the frontal esophageal ligament using an advanced bipolar device. Dissection continues around the gastroesophageal junction, creating a retroesophageal window through gentle blunt dissection. A Penrose drain encircles the esophagus for non-traumatic retraction. The phrenoesophageal ligament is completely divided, allowing circumferential mobilization of the distal esophagus to achieve adequate intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The surgeon closes the hiatus posteriorly using interrupted stitches with zero silk, placing each suture one centimeter apart. A calibration bougie is used to ensure the closure is not too tight. Bioabsorbable pledges are included for reinforcement, and the slipped knot technique is applied, following standard suturing principles.

The surgeon uses regular laparoscopic scissors to cut sutures, avoiding the use of robotic shears. The anterior closure is adjusted by removing a constricting stitch and placing an additional suture posteriorly. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to verify the passage of the endoscope, assess the GEJ position, intraabdominal esophageal length, and mucosal integrity. A pneumatic leak test is conducted, confirming no fundoplication due to ineffective esophageal motility.
A round-blade drain is placed in the mediastinum. The surgeon performs a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy, followed by closure of the port sites using standard techniques.
